abs.description:
Returns the absolute value of X. If X is a string or blob, it returns 0.

SELECT abs(-5);  --> 5
SELECT abs("-3");  --> 3
SELECT abs("libsql");  --> 0

avg.description: The avg() function returns the average of all non-NULL values in a group, interpreting non-numeric strings and BLOBs as 0. It always yields a floating point result if there is at least one non-NULL input and returns NULL if there are no non-NULL inputs.

changes.description: The changes() function returns the number of rows altered by the latest INSERT, DELETE, or UPDATE statement, excluding changes from lower-level triggers.

char.description:
The char(X1,X2,…,XN) function returns a string composed of characters having the unicode code point values of integers X1 through XN, respectively.

SELECT CHAR(65, 66, 67);
-> 'ABC'

coalesce.description:
The coalesce() function returns a copy of its first non-NULL argument, or NULL if all arguments are NULL. Coalesce() must have at least 2 arguments.

select coalesce(null, 50);
-> 50

concat.description:
The concat function returns a string formed by concatenating all its non-NULL arguments. If all arguments are NULL, it returns an empty string.

select concat('hello', ' ', 'world')
-> 'hello world'

concat_ws.description:
The concat_ws concatenates all non-null arguments beyond the first, using the first argument as a separator. If the first argument is NULL, it returns NULL. If all other arguments are NULL, it returns an empty string.

select concat_ws(', ', 'hello', 'world')
-> 'hello world'

count.description: The count(X) function returns a count of the number of times that X is not NULL in a group. The count(*) function (with no arguments) returns the total number of rows in the group.

format.description:
The FORMAT() function, similar to C's printf(), uses a format string (first argument) to construct the output with values from subsequent arguments.

select format('i am %d years old', 50);
-> 'i am 50 years old'

fts5.description:
FTS5 is an SQLite virtual table module that provides full-text search functionality to database applications.

CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE movie_fts USING fts5(title, summary);
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE name_fts USING fts5(name, tokenize='trigram');

External Content Tables

CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE student_fts USING fts5(
    name,
    tokenize='trigram',
    content='student',
    content_rowid='id'
);

glob.description:
The GLOB operator is like LIKE but uses Unix file globbing syntax and is case-sensitive.

select glob('*hello*', 'hello world');
-> 1

group_concat.description: The function concatenates all non-NULL values of X into a string, using Y as the separator. If Y is omitted, a comma (",") is used.

hex.description:
The hex() function converts its BLOB argument into an upper-case hexadecimal string.

select hex(x'ffeeaa');
-> FFEEAA

ifnull.description:
The ifnull() function returns the first non-NULL argument, or NULL if both are NULL. It requires exactly 2 arguments and is equivalent to coalesce() with two arguments.

select ifnull(null, 5);
-> 5

iif.description:
The iif(X,Y,Z) function returns the value Y if X is true, and Z otherwise

select iif(age >= 18, 'adult', 'underage');
-> 'underage'

instr.description:
The instr(X, Y) function returns the position of the first occurrence of string Y within string X plus 1, or 0 if Y is not found in X.

select instr('hello world', 'wo');
-> 7

last_insert_rowid.description: The lastinsertrowid() function returns the ROWID of the last row insert from the database connection which invoked the function.

length.description:
The length(X) function returns the character count of string X, excluding any NUL characters for strings (which SQLite typically lacks), or the byte count for blobs. If X is NULL, length(X) is also NULL. For numeric X, it returns the length of its string representation.

select length('hello');
-> 5

select length(x'ff00ee');
-> 3

select length(NULL);
-> NULL

libsql_vector_idx.description:
Use the libsqlvectoridx expression in the CREATE INDEX statement to create an ANN index.

CREATE INDEX movies_idx ON movies (libsql_vector_idx(embedding));

like.description:
The like() function checks if the string Y matches the pattern X in the "Y LIKE X [ESCAPE Z]" expression.

select like('hel%', 'hello')
-> 1

select like('wor%', 'hello')
-> 0

lower.description:
The lower(X) function converts all ASCII characters in string X to lowercase.

select lower('Hello World');
-> 'hello world'

ltrim.description:
The ltrim(X, Y) function removes characters specified in Y from the left side of string X. Omitting Y removes spaces from the left side of X.

select ltrim(' hello');
-> 'hello'

select ltrim('0.005', '0.');
-> '5'

max.description:
The multi-argument max() function returns the maximum value among its arguments, or NULL if any argument is NULL. It uses the collating function of the first argument for string comparisons, defaulting to the BINARY collating function if none is specified. When given a single argument, max() acts as an aggregate function.

select max(5, 6, 1);
-> 6

select max(age) from users;

min.description:
The multi-argument min() function returns the minimum value among its arguments, utilizing the collating function of the first argument for string comparisons or defaulting to BINARY. When given a single argument, min() acts as an aggregate function.

select min(5, 6, 1);
-> 1

select min(age) from users;

nullif.description:
The nullif(X, Y) function returns X if X and Y are different, and NULL if they are the same.

select nullif(6, 6)
-> NULL

select nullif(7, 6)
-> 7

octet_length.description:
The octet_length(X) function returns the number of bytes in the encoding of text string X. If X is NULL, it returns NULL. For BLOBs, it returns the same as length(X). For numeric values, it returns the byte count of the text representation.

select octet_length('វិសាល'); -> 15
select octet_length('visal'); -> 5
select octet_length(x'ff11ee');  -> 3

printf.description:
The printf() function, similar to C's printf(), uses a format string (first argument) to construct the output with values from subsequent arguments.

select printf('i am %d years old', 50);
-> 'i am 50 years old'

quote.description:
The quote(X) function returns the SQL literal for X, suitable for inclusion in an SQL statement. Strings are single-quoted with escaped interior quotes. BLOBs are encoded as hex literals.

select quote('hello '' world')
-> 'hello '' world'

select quote(x'ffee00')
-> X'FFEE00'

random.description: The random() function returns a pseudo-random integer between -9223372036854775808 and +9223372036854775807.

randomblob.description:
The randomblob(N) function returns an N-byte blob of pseudo-random bytes. If N is less than 1, it returns a 1-byte random blob.

select hex(randomblob(3))
-> 35DD3E

replace.description:
The replace(X, Y, Z) function returns X with every occurrence of Y replaced by Z.

select replace('hello world', 'world', 'planet')
-> 'hello planet'

round.description:
The round(X, Y) function returns X rounded to Y decimal places. If Y is omitted or negative, it defaults to 0.

select round(1.6);
-> 2

select round(1.665, 2);
-> 1.67

rtrim.description:
The rtrim(X, Y) function removes all characters in Y from the right side of X. If Y is omitted, it removes spaces.

select rtrim('hello ');
-> 'hello'

select rtrim('5,000', '0,');
-> '5'

sign.description:
The sign(X) function returns -1, 0, or 1 if X is negative, zero, or positive, respectively. If X is NULL or not a number, it returns NULL.

select sign(-5);  -> -1
select sign(0);   -> 0
select sign(5);   -> 1

string_agg.description: The function concatenates all non-NULL values of X into a string, using Y as the separator. If Y is omitted, a comma (",") is used.

sum.description: The function returns the sum of all non-NULL values in the group, returning NULL if there are no non-NULL inputs.

total.description: The function returns the sum of all non-NULL values in the group, with a result of 0.0 if there are no non-NULL inputs. The result of total() is always a floating point value.

vector.description:
Function to convert a vector from string format to binary.

INSERT INTO movies (title, year, embedding) VALUES('Napoleon', 2023, vector('[1,2,3]'));

vector_distance_cos.description:
Function to calculate cosine distance between two vectors. It computes the distance as 1 minus the cosine similarity, meaning a smaller distance indicates closer vectors.

SELECT * FROM movie
ORDER BY vector_distance_cos(embedding, '[3,1,2]')

vector_extract.description:
Function to extract string from binary vector

SELECT title,
  vector_extract(embedding),
  vector_distance_cos(embedding, vector('[5,6,7]'))
FROM movies;

vector_top_k.description:
Use vectortopk with the idxname index to efficiently find the top k most similar vectors to qvector

SELECT title, year
FROM vector_top_k('movies_idx', vector('[4,5,6]'), 3)
JOIN movies ON movies.rowid = id

json.description:
The json() function verifies that its argument is a valid JSON string and returns a minified version of that JSON string (with all unnecessary spaces and tabs removed). If the argument is not a valid JSON string, the function throws an error.

SELECT json('{"a": 1, "b": 2}');
-> '{"a":1,"b":2}'

SELECT json('[1, 2, 3]');
-> '[1,2,3]'

jsonb.description:
The jsonb() function works like json() except that it returns a BLOB in the binary JSONB format instead of a text string. The JSONB format is more compact and faster to parse than text JSON.

SELECT hex(jsonb('{"a": 1, "b": 2}'));
-> 'A264A16101B16202'

json_array.description:
The json_array() function accepts zero or more arguments and returns a JSON array composed of those arguments. NULL arguments are rendered as JSON null values.

SELECT json_array(1, 2, 'three', NULL);
-> '[1,2,"three",null]'

SELECT json_array();
-> '[]'

jsonb_array.description:
The jsonb_array() function works like json_array() except that it returns a BLOB in the binary JSONB format.

SELECT hex(jsonb_array(1, 2, 'three'));
-> 'A30101026574687265650203'

json_array_length.description:
The json_array_length() function returns the number of elements in the JSON array, or 0 if the JSON value is not an array. If the optional path argument is provided, it returns the length of the array at that path.

SELECT json_array_length('[1,2,3,4]');
-> 4

SELECT json_array_length('{"a":[1,2,3]}', '$.a');
-> 3

SELECT json_array_length('{}');
-> 0

json_error_position.description:
The json_error_position() function returns the position of the first syntax error in the JSON string, or 0 if the JSON is valid.

SELECT json_error_position('{"a":1,"b":2}');
-> 0

SELECT json_error_position('{"a":1,"b":}');
-> 12

json_extract.description:
The json_extract() function extracts values from a JSON string using JSONPath expressions. Multiple paths can be provided to extract multiple values.

SELECT json_extract('{"a":2,"c":[4,5]}', '$.a');
-> 2

SELECT json_extract('{"a":2,"c":[4,5]}', '$.c[0]');
-> 4

SELECT json_extract('{"a":2,"c":[4,5]}', '$.a', '$.c');
-> 2

jsonb_extract.description:
The jsonb_extract() function works like json_extract() except that it accepts JSONB as the first argument and returns JSONB values.

SELECT jsonb_extract(jsonb('{"a":2,"c":[4,5]}'), '$.a');
-> 2

json_insert.description:
The json_insert() function inserts values into a JSON string without overwriting existing values. It takes pairs of path and value arguments.

SELECT json_insert('{"a":1}', '$.b', 2);
-> '{"a":1,"b":2}'

SELECT json_insert('{"a":1}', '$.a', 2);
-> '{"a":1}'

jsonb_insert.description:
The jsonb_insert() function works like json_insert() except that it returns a BLOB in the binary JSONB format.

SELECT hex(jsonb_insert(jsonb('{"a":1}'), '$.b', 2));
-> 'A264A16101B16202'

json_object.description:
The json_object() function accepts zero or more pairs of arguments and returns a JSON object composed of those arguments. The first argument of each pair is the label and the second is the value.

SELECT json_object('a', 1, 'b', 2);
-> '{"a":1,"b":2}'

SELECT json_object();
-> '{}'

jsonb_object.description:
The jsonb_object() function works like json_object() except that it returns a BLOB in the binary JSONB format.

SELECT hex(jsonb_object('a', 1, 'b', 2));
-> 'A264A16101B16202'

json_patch.description:
The json_patch() function applies an RFC 7396 JSON Merge Patch to json1 using the patch data in json2.

SELECT json_patch('{"a":1,"b":2}', '{"b":3,"c":4}');
-> '{"a":1,"b":3,"c":4}'

SELECT json_patch('{"a":1,"b":2}', '{"b":null}');
-> '{"a":1}'

jsonb_patch.description:
The jsonb_patch() function works like json_patch() except that it returns a BLOB in the binary JSONB format.

SELECT hex(jsonb_patch(jsonb('{"a":1}'), jsonb('{"b":2}')));
-> 'A264A16101B16202'

json_pretty.description:
The json_pretty() function returns a pretty-printed version of the JSON string with proper indentation and line breaks.

SELECT json_pretty('{"a":1,"b":[2,3]}');
->
'{
  "a": 1,
  "b": [
    2,
    3
  ]
}'

json_remove.description:
The json_remove() function removes elements from a JSON string at the specified paths.

SELECT json_remove('{"a":1,"b":2,"c":3}', '$.b');
-> '{"a":1,"c":3}'

SELECT json_remove('[1,2,3,4]', '$[1]', '$[2]');
-> '[1,4]'

jsonb_remove.description:
The jsonb_remove() function works like json_remove() except that it returns a BLOB in the binary JSONB format.

SELECT hex(jsonb_remove(jsonb('{"a":1,"b":2}'), '$.b'));
-> 'A164A16101'

json_replace.description:
The json_replace() function replaces existing values in a JSON string without adding new values. It takes pairs of path and value arguments.

SELECT json_replace('{"a":1,"b":2}', '$.a', 'hello');
-> '{"a":"hello","b":2}'

SELECT json_replace('{"a":1}', '$.b', 2);
-> '{"a":1}'

jsonb_replace.description:
The jsonb_replace() function works like json_replace() except that it returns a BLOB in the binary JSONB format.

SELECT hex(jsonb_replace(jsonb('{"a":1}'), '$.a', 'hello'));
-> 'A164A1656865726C6C6F05'

json_set.description:
The json_set() function inserts or replaces values in a JSON string. It takes pairs of path and value arguments.

SELECT json_set('{"a":1}', '$.a', 2);
-> '{"a":2}'

SELECT json_set('{"a":1}', '$.b', 2);
-> '{"a":1,"b":2}'

jsonb_set.description:
The jsonb_set() function works like json_set() except that it returns a BLOB in the binary JSONB format.

SELECT hex(jsonb_set(jsonb('{"a":1}'), '$.b', 2));
-> 'A264A16101B16202'

json_type.description:
The json_type() function returns the type of the JSON value as a string. Possible return values are 'null', 'true', 'false', 'integer', 'real', 'text', 'array', and 'object'.

SELECT json_type('[1,2,3]');
-> 'array'

SELECT json_type('{"a":1}', '$.a');
-> 'integer'

SELECT json_type('"hello"');
-> 'text'

json_valid.description:
The json_valid() function returns 1 if the argument is valid JSON, and 0 otherwise. The optional flags parameter allows checking for specific JSON formats.

SELECT json_valid('{"a":1}');
-> 1

SELECT json_valid('{"a":1,}');
-> 0

SELECT json_valid('{a:1}', 6);
-> 1

json_quote.description:
The json_quote() function converts an SQL value into its JSON representation. Strings are quoted and escaped, numbers are converted to JSON numbers.

SELECT json_quote('hello');
-> '"hello"'

SELECT json_quote(123);
-> '123'

SELECT json_quote(NULL);
-> 'null'

json_group_array.description:
The json_group_array() is an aggregate function that returns a JSON array composed of all values in the group.

SELECT json_group_array(name) FROM users;
-> '["Alice","Bob","Charlie"]'

jsonb_group_array.description:
The jsonb_group_array() is an aggregate function that works like json_group_array() except that it returns a BLOB in the binary JSONB format.

SELECT hex(jsonb_group_array(name)) FROM users;
-> 'A305416C69636503426F6205436861726C696503'

json_group_object.description:
The json_group_object() is an aggregate function that returns a JSON object composed of all name/value pairs in the group.

SELECT json_group_object(name, age) FROM users;
-> '{"Alice":25,"Bob":30,"Charlie":35}'

jsonb_group_object.description:
The jsonb_group_object() is an aggregate function that works like json_group_object() except that it returns a BLOB in the binary JSONB format.

SELECT hex(jsonb_group_object(name, age)) FROM users;
-> 'A3456C69636519426F621E436861726C696523'

json_each.description:
The json_each() table-valued function walks through the immediate children of a JSON array or object and returns one row for each element. It returns columns: key, value, type, atom, id, parent, fullkey, path.

SELECT * FROM json_each('{"a":1,"b":2}');
-- Returns:
-- key | value | type    | atom | ...
-- a   | 1     | integer | 1    | ...
-- b   | 2     | integer | 2    | ...

json_tree.description:
The json_tree() table-valued function recursively walks through all elements of a JSON structure starting from the top level. It returns the same columns as json_each() but includes all nested elements.

SELECT fullkey, value FROM json_tree('{"a":{"b":1}}');
-- Returns:
-- fullkey | value
-- $       | {"a":{"b":1}}
-- $.a     | {"b":1}
-- $.a.b   | 1
